audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel / Shemuel Bet chapter 2, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Hebron inquiry, household migration, Judah's anointing, Jabesh-gilead messaging, Abner's installation of Ish-bosheth, divided chronology, the Gibeon pool contest, Asahel's pursuit and death, casualty counts, and burial.

verses.restored: After this David inquired of YHWH, saying, Shall I go up into one of cities of Judah? YHWH said to him, Go up. David said, To where shall I go up? YHWH said, To Hebron.
David went up there, and also his two wives, Ahinoam the Jezreelite and Abigail wife of Nabal the Carmelite.
David brought up his men who were with him, each man and his household, and they lived in cities of Hebron.
Men of Judah came and anointed David there as king over house of Judah. They told David, saying, Men of Jabesh-gilead were those who buried Saul.
David sent messengers to men of Jabesh-gilead and said to them, Blessed are you to YHWH, because you did this covenant-kindness with your lord, with Saul, and buried him.
Now may YHWH do covenant-kindness and faithfulness with you. I also will do this good with you, because you have done this thing.
Now let your hands be strong, and be children of strength, because Saul your lord is dead, and also house of Judah has anointed me king over them.
But Abner son of Ner, commander of Saul's army, took Ish-bosheth son of Saul and brought him over to Mahanaim.
He made him king over Gilead, Ashurites, Jezreel, Ephraim, Benjamin, and over all Israel.
Ish-bosheth son of Saul was forty years old when he became king over Israel, and he reigned two years. Only house of Judah followed David.
Number of days David was king in Hebron over house of Judah was seven years and six months.
Abner son of Ner and servants of Ish-bosheth son of Saul went out from Mahanaim to Gibeon.
Joab son of Zeruiah and servants of David went out and met them at pool of Gibeon together. These sat on one side of pool, and those sat on other side of pool.
Abner said to Joab, Please let young men arise and play/fight before us. Joab said, Let them arise.
They arose and crossed over by number: twelve for Benjamin and Ish-bosheth son of Saul, and twelve from servants of David.
Each grasped head of his companion and thrust his sword into side of his companion, and they fell together. Therefore that place was called Field of Sharp Blades, which is in Gibeon.
Battle was very severe on that day, and Abner and men of Israel were struck before servants of David.
Three sons of Zeruiah were there: Joab, Abishai, and Asahel. Asahel was swift in his feet like one gazelle that is in field.
Asahel pursued after Abner and did not turn to go right or left from after Abner.
Abner turned behind him and said, Is this you, Asahel? He said, It is I.
Abner said to him, Turn aside to your right or to your left, seize for yourself one of young men, and take his equipment for yourself. But Asahel was not willing to turn aside from after him.
Abner again said to Asahel, Turn aside from after me. Why should I strike you to ground? How then could I lift my face to Joab your brother?

But he refused to turn aside. Abner struck him in abdomen with rear of spear, and spear came out behind him. He fell there and died in his place. All who came to place where Asahel fell and died stood still.
Joab and Abishai pursued after Abner. Sun was going down when they came to hill of Ammah, which is before Giah by way of wilderness of Gibeon.
Children of Benjamin gathered after Abner and became one band, and they stood on top of one hill.
Abner called to Joab and said, Shall sword devour forever? Do you not know that it will be bitter in latter end? How long will you not say to people to return from after their brothers?
Joab said, As Divine lives, if you had not spoken, surely only in morning would people have gone up, each from pursuing his brother.
Joab blew shofar, and all people stood still. They no longer pursued after Israel, and they did not continue to fight.
Abner and his men walked through Arabah all that night, crossed Jordan, walked through all Bithron, and came to Mahanaim.
Joab returned from after Abner and gathered all people. From servants of David nineteen men were missing, and Asahel.
Servants of David had struck from Benjamin and among men of Abner three hundred sixty men who died.
They lifted Asahel and buried him in tomb of his father, which was in Bethlehem. Joab and his men walked all night, and daylight came upon them at Hebron.

translation_notes: David's move to Hebron includes wives, soldiers, dependents, and whole households. Political transition is lived by communities, not by a solitary hero.
David's message to Jabesh-gilead honors burial work while also functioning as an appeal to a city loyal to Saul. Piety and political strategy coexist.
Ish-bosheth is installed through Abner's military power. His name may reflect a later shame-substitution for Eshbaal; textual and naming traditions require audit.
The reign lengths and territorial claims create chronological tensions. The data should not be smoothed into false precision.
The pool encounter called 'play' is a lethal staged contest in which twenty-four young men kill one another. It is not sport, entertainment, or a model of honorable masculinity.
Asahel's speed is a bodily trait, not proof of invulnerability or moral superiority. Abner warns him twice before killing him, but battlefield warning does not cleanse the wider civil war.
Casualty imbalance may function as royal propaganda. Every counted death represents a person within a divided people.
Abner's appeal to brotherhood stops the pursuit only after extensive killing. Shared identity should prevent violence earlier, not merely conclude it.
safety_note: Maximum safeguards: Do not romanticize the Gibeon contest, civil war, pursuit, or casualty arithmetic. Do not treat providential inquiry as permission for dynastic conquest. Center households, young fighters used by commanders, the dead on both sides, and the political interests behind competing anointings.
